statement: Narrowing a column type (TEXT → VARCHAR(100), BIGINT → INT, NUMERIC → INTEGER) silently truncates or fails on existing rows that exceed the new bound.
condition: The migration runs against a database with existing rows that violate the new constraint. Behavior depends on the database engine: some truncate (MySQL with default settings), some fail mid-migration leaving the table in a partial state, some succeed but corrupt subsequent reads.
frequency: Whenever schema authors guess at column sizes without checking the actual data distribution.
current_mitigation: Manual query of MAX(LENGTH(column)) before authoring the migration.
not_fixed_because: The check is easy to skip when the migration is mechanical.
safe_pattern: Before narrowing: run `SELECT MAX(LENGTH(column)), MAX(column), MIN(column) FROM table` against production (or a recent production snapshot). If any value exceeds the proposed new bound, either pick a wider type, or split into a backfill-then-narrow migration that explicitly handles the out-of-bound rows (truncation, rejection, archival). Add a CHECK constraint as part of the same migration to prevent future violations.
history: Common incident: NUMERIC(10,2) → NUMERIC(8,2) on a financial column. The migration succeeded because no historical row exceeded the bound, but a subsequent business event (a single large transaction) caused a runtime error that took down the payment write path for 40 minutes.
